facts_disclosed: Does ballet twice a week — been dancing since childhood in Russia

turns.content: do you have any advice for dealing with muscle soreness that isn't just "take ibuprofen and shut up"? because my body is screaming at me right now

I can definitely help with that. First -- what's causing the soreness? Is it from exercise, physical work, or something else? And where specifically are you feeling it? That'll help me give you something more useful than the ibuprofen advice.

combination of things. I'm on my feet 12-14 hours a day at work and then I do ballet twice a week on top of that. my calves and feet are basically in a permanent state of protest. Last night after barre work I could barely walk home

That's a serious amount of physical demand -- long hours on your feet for work plus ballet on top of it. Your calves and feet are essentially never getting a full recovery window. A few things that actually help beyond ibuprofen:

**Immediate relief:**
- Epsom salt soaks for your feet -- 15-20 minutes in warm water with a generous amount. The magnesium absorbs through the skin and helps with muscle relaxation.
- A frozen water bottle rolled under your feet. The cold reduces inflammation and the rolling massages the plantar fascia.

**Recovery between sessions:**
- Foam rolling your calves -- spend at least two minutes per leg, slow and deliberate. It hurts but it works.
- Compression socks during your long shifts at work. They look unglamorous but the difference in how your legs feel after 12 hours is significant.

**Longer term:**
- Are you stretching after ballet, or are you rushing out? Post-class stretching when the muscles are warm makes a huge difference in how you feel the next day.

How long have you been doing ballet?

since I was five actually. I started in Petersburg — it's one of those things where every little Russian girl gets put in ballet and most quit but I never did. It's been the one constant through everything. Moving countries, school, career... ballet stayed

Nearly thirty years of ballet -- that's not a hobby, that's part of who you are. The fact that you've maintained it through major life changes says a lot. Your body has a deep foundation of that training even if it's protesting the combined workload right now.

With that kind of history, your muscle memory and technique are strong, which actually means your soreness is probably more about recovery deficit than form issues. Your body knows the movements -- it just needs more support between sessions.

A few more things given your background:

- **Active recovery days** -- on days between ballet, gentle movement like walking or light stretching is better than total rest. Your body is conditioned for movement; going completely still can actually make the stiffness worse.
- **Protein timing** -- are you eating enough protein after your sessions? Ballet is demanding on the muscles and if you're not refueling properly, recovery takes much longer.
- **Sleep** -- this is the biggest one. How much are you getting? With long work hours, that can be the weak link.

sleep is a disaster honestly. I work evenings so I don't get home until after midnight most nights and then I'm too wired to sleep. My brain is still plating dishes at 2am. And then ballet class is at 10 in the morning so on those days I'm running on maybe five hours

Five hours of sleep with your physical demands -- that's where a lot of the soreness problem lives. Your muscles repair during deep sleep, and if you're consistently getting five hours, you're never fully recovering. You're essentially starting each day with a recovery debt that compounds.

I know the wired-after-work feeling is hard to fight, especially if your job is high-intensity. A few things that can help you wind down faster:

- **No screens for 30 minutes before bed.** I know, everyone says this, but the blue light genuinely suppresses melatonin.
- **A cool shower** after you get home -- it drops your core body temperature, which signals your body to sleep. Not cold, just cool.
- **Magnesium glycinate** before bed -- 200-400mg. It supports both muscle recovery and sleep quality. Two birds.
- **A brain dump** -- keep a notebook by your bed and spend five minutes writing down whatever is still spinning in your head. Tomorrow's prep list, things you need to fix, whatever. Getting it out of your head and onto paper can break the mental loop.

On ballet days, is there any way to push your class to an afternoon slot so you can sleep in?

there's no afternoon class at my studio unfortunately, they only have morning and evening and evening conflicts with work obviously. The magnesium thing — someone else told me about that too. Maybe I should actually try it. The brain dump idea though, that's interesting. Because yes my head is literally going through every plate from service. Did that sauce reduce enough. Was the fish overcooked on table 12. It never stops

That mental replay is incredibly common in high-pressure jobs. Your brain is essentially running quality control after the fact, which is useful during work but counterproductive at 2am when you can't change anything.

The notebook works because it externalizes the loop. Write it down: "table 12 fish -- check timing tomorrow," "sauce needed another two minutes." Once it's on paper, your brain gets the signal that it's been captured and it can let go. You don't even need to look at the notes the next day -- the act of writing is what matters.

And yes, try the magnesium. It's one of those things that sounds too simple to work but the evidence is solid. Start with 200mg and see how you feel after a week. Between the magnesium and the brain dump, you might gain an extra hour of sleep, and that hour will make a noticeable difference in how your calves feel after ballet.

Are you doing anything for nutrition specifically around your ballet sessions? With a morning class and your work schedule, I'm guessing you might be dancing on an empty stomach.

guilty. I usually just have coffee before class because I can't eat that early. Then I eat something after but honestly sometimes I just go straight to work and eat whatever I can grab between prep tasks

Dancing on coffee alone and then not eating properly until you're already at work -- that's a recipe for the exact soreness and fatigue you're describing. Your muscles are working hard in class with no fuel and then not getting the rebuilding materials they need afterward.

You don't need a full meal before a 10am class, but something small can make a big difference:

- A banana with a spoonful of peanut butter -- quick carbs plus a little protein and fat. Takes thirty seconds.
- Or a small handful of nuts and a few dates. Easy to eat even if your appetite isn't awake yet.

After class, within 45 minutes ideally, you need protein. A quick option: Greek yogurt with some granola. You can eat it in the car or on the way to work. The post-exercise window is when your muscles are most receptive to repair, and if you miss it consistently, the soreness accumulates.

I know with your schedule it feels impossible, but even small adjustments here will compound over time.

the banana thing I can do. That's easy enough. You know what's funny — I'm so disciplined about food at work. Every component timed perfectly, every protein temp checked. But for myself? I eat like a university student half the time. Blyat

That's not unusual at all -- professionals in food often eat the worst when it comes to their own meals. You spend all your energy and focus on other people's plates and then run on adrenaline and scraps yourself.

But think of it this way: your body is your primary tool. You wouldn't let your knives get dull or your station get messy. The same discipline you bring to the kitchen applies here. Feed yourself like you'd feed a VIP table -- or at least like you'd feed a decent regular.

So the plan: banana before ballet, protein after, magnesium before bed, notebook on the nightstand, and compression socks during work shifts. None of this is complicated -- it's just consistency. Same as ballet, same as your job.

feed myself like a decent regular. Ha. That's a low bar but honestly it's higher than where I'm at. Also the compression socks — I tried those once and they looked ridiculous with my kitchen clogs. But if they work...

Nobody in a kitchen is winning fashion awards. The clog-and-compression-sock combination is not going to land you a magazine cover, but your legs at hour twelve will feel dramatically different. The reduced swelling and fatigue is real -- there's solid research behind it for people who stand for extended periods.

Try them for one full shift. If the difference is noticeable, vanity stops being a factor very quickly. Most nurses and surgeons wear them daily for the same reason.

you're right. And honestly ballet is the only thing that keeps me sane. It's the only time my body moves without me thinking about food. Everything else in my life revolves around the kitchen. Ballet is just... movement and music and discipline. The discipline is the same as cooking actually — repetition until it's perfect — but the headspace is completely different

That's a really important distinction. Ballet gives you the physical rigor you clearly thrive on but in a completely different mental mode. It's meditative in a way that a kitchen can't be -- no tickets, no timing, no one waiting for a plate. Just you and the movement.

Protecting that practice means protecting the recovery that makes it sustainable. If you burn out physically because you're not sleeping or eating properly, ballet is the first thing that'll suffer, and then you lose the one thing that's giving you mental relief.

So take care of the body that does both jobs. Start small -- the banana, the magnesium, the notebook. See how you feel after two weeks. I think you'll notice a real difference.

two weeks. D'accord. I'll try it. If the magnesium works I'll owe you one. Now I need to go to sleep because I have class in eight hours and this is exactly the problem we just talked about haha. Merci
Go sleep. And put the phone down -- that counts as screen time. Banana in the morning, class at ten, and maybe start the magnesium tomorrow night. Your calves will thank you by next week. Bonne nuit.
